messages.content: isn't it wild how a coffee that was like 2 bucks when i was a teenager is basically 6 now

Yeah, it really does hit different when you think about it that way! There's something about coffee prices specifically that people notice because it's such a daily ritual for so many people.

A few things are going on there - general inflation over the years, but also coffee has kind of **shifted culturally** into more of a "premium experience" thing, especially with the rise of places like Starbucks setting a new normal for what people expect to pay.

How long ago are we talking? Like the 90s, early 2000s? Because that's actually a pretty significant jump even accounting for normal inflation. Some of it is genuine cost increases - labor, rent for those locations, supply chain stuff - but honestly some of it is also just... **people got used to paying more** and the industry figured that out.

Do you still buy coffee out regularly or has the price pushed you toward making it at home more?
i was telling my coworker, feels like everything just quietly crept up and we all kinda accepted it

That's such a good way to put it - "quietly crept up." It really does happen gradually enough that there's no single moment where you go "okay THIS is too much" and push back collectively.

It's almost like the **boiling frog thing** - each individual price increase is small enough to absorb, so you just... adjust. And then you look back and realize your whole cost of living shifted significantly without you ever really deciding to accept it.

And there's something almost social about it too - if everyone around you is still buying the $6 coffee, it feels weird to be the one making a big deal of it. So the acceptance kind of spreads person to person without anyone really choosing it consciously.

Your coworker probably felt that same recognition right? Like it's one of those observations that once someone says it out loud, everyone goes "yeah... wait, yeah exactly."

It's interesting timing too - lunchtime on a workday, these conversations happen naturally when you're just going about your day and something small triggers that bigger realization. Did it come up because you were actually grabbing coffee or just randomly?
makes me a little nostalgic for the days when a 20 in your pocket felt like real money lol

Ha, right?! A $20 used to feel like you were genuinely set for the day - grab food, drinks, maybe still have change left over. Now it's like... that's basically one decent meal and you're done.

There's something kind of bittersweet about that nostalgia though. Part of it is probably the money itself, but part of it might also just be nostalgia for that feeling of being younger when small things felt more abundant and carefree? Like a $20 when you're a teenager with not many responsibilities hits different than a $20 as an adult with bills and everything weighing on you.

Though the math is also just genuinely real - purchasing power has actually dropped significantly for a lot of everyday stuff, so it's not just nostalgia tricking you.

Do you find yourself actually changing your habits much because of it, or is it more just that background hum of mild frustration that you've kind of made peace with?
anyway not complaining really, just one of those shower thoughts about how prices change over time

Haha yeah those shower thought observations are some of the best ones - not really a complaint, just your brain going "huh, weird how that works" and then you share it and everyone immediately relates.

It's actually kind of a fun thing to think about in a bigger picture way - like prices and what things "feel worth" is so tied to whatever era you grew up in. Your mental baseline just gets set early and never fully updates.

Anyway hope the rest of your workday goes smoothly - you've got lunch ahead of you at least! 😄
